news_summary: Recent space discoveries reveal new insights into early life’s chemistry, uncover a scorching super-Earth resembling Mercury, and identify an unexpectedly atmospheric tiny outer Solar System world.
news_summary_fr: De récentes découvertes spatiales apportent un nouvel éclairage sur la chimie des débuts de la vie, révèlent l'existence d'une super-Terre brûlante ressemblant à Mercure et mettent en évidence un minuscule monde de la périphérie du système solaire doté d'une atmosphère inattendue.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos espaciales aportan nuevos conocimientos sobre la química de los orígenes de la vida, revelan una super-Tierra abrasadora parecida a Mercurio e identifican un pequeño mundo del sistema solar exterior con una atmósfera inesperada.

articles.title: NASA Research Shows Early Life Relied on Rare Metal
articles.summary: NASA-funded scientists have discovered that life on Earth over 3 billion years ago relied on the metal molybdenum, which was incredibly scarce in the environment at the time. The study, published in Nature Communications on Tuesday, is the first to show that molybdenum was used by ancient life this far back in our planet’s history. […]
articles.content:
Timeline of Earth’s history in billions of years. The new study indicates that life used molybdenum as far back as 3.3 to 3.7 billion years ago, long before levels of molybdenum in the oceans increased to modern levels. Other events in Earth’s history are marked for context. NASA

NASA-funded scientists have discovered that life on Earth over 3 billion years ago relied on the metal molybdenum, which was incredibly scarce in the environment at the time. The study, published in Nature Communications on Tuesday, is the first to show that molybdenum was used by ancient life this far back in our planet’s history.

On Earth today, molybdenum helps speed up vital biochemical reactions in cells. The metal is a component of essential enzymes that drive several major biological reactions in organisms. This is not only important for the individual organisms, but also biogeochemical cycles, such as the nitrogen cycle, which affect our entire planet. Without molybdenum, those important reactions could still happen in nature, but they would be too slow to sustain life.

“Molybdenum sits at the catalytic center of enzymes that run major carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur reactions,” explained Betül Kaçar, head of the Kaçar Lab at the University of Wisconsin-Madison and senior author on the study. Kaçar leads MUSE, a NASA Interdisciplinary Consortia for Astrobiology Research (ICAR) at UW-Madison.

“Asking when life began using molybdenum is really asking when some of the most consequential metabolic strategies became possible,” said Kaçar.

Molybdenum through history

Molybdenum is now relatively common in the environment, and its scarcity is no longer a problem for life. But that wasn’t always the case.

Geological evidence shows that only trace amounts of molybdenum were present in Earth’s oceans billions of years ago. Levels increased around the time that microorganisms began to use photosynthesis, which eventually led to a dramatic boost in the amount of atmospheric oxygen (roughly 2.45 billion years ago). This is known as the Great Oxidation Event and had a profound effect on the evolution of life. A previous NASA study even suggested that the rise of molybdenum in the environment around this time may have been necessary for the evolution of complex life.

But when did life first start using molybdenum? Because of its scarcity on ancient Earth, astrobiologists have wondered if life could have started by using other metals to speed along vital reactions. Tungsten, for instance, behaves similarly in cells and is used today by some organisms that live in extreme environments. Scientists previously theorized that life may have used tungsten first and then evolved to used molybdenum once it became more available. The new study shows this wasn’t necessarily the case.

The team gathered available data on the prevalence of molybdenum through time and reconstructed the history of the metal’s use along the branches of the tree of life. They found that although molybdenum was scarce, ancient microbes on Earth still found a way to use it. The same is true for the use of the metal tungsten.

“Our work shows that both molybdenum and tungsten-using enzyme systems have Archean roots, which suggests that early life likely worked with both metals rather than following a simple “tungsten first, molybdenum later” story,” said Kaçar. “We argue that molybdenum use is far older than many models assumed, with molecular dating placing molybdenum utilization back into the Eoarchean to Mesoarchean, roughly 3.7–3.1 billion years ago, well before the Great Oxidation Event.”

Accessing molybdenum

Previous work from the MUSE ICAR, published in 2024, identified certain niches where early life may have found supplies of molybdenum and other scarce metals deep below the oceans. Hydrothermal vents at the seafloor provide trace metals including iron, zinc, copper, nickel, manganese, vanadium, molybdenum, cobalt, and tungsten.

“Even if Archean seawater held little dissolved molybdenum overall, localized systems such as hydrothermal vents could still have supplied usable amounts of molybdenum and other metals,” said Kaçar.

The new study shows that, even amid an assortment of other useful metals, molybdenum was somehow one of life’s first choices as a metal catalyst.

“Molybdenum may have been worth “choosing” because it enables catalysis across a broad range of substrates and redox conditions,” said Kaçar. “In other words, scarcity did not make molybdenum unimportant; its catalytic advantages may have made it worth evolving ways to acquire and use.”

The study shows how life can find a way to use elements in the environment, even if they are scarce, and reminds us that in the search for life beyond Earth we must be prepared for possibilities that we haven’t yet considered.

Bio-essential elements, search for life in universe

Searching for life in the universe isn’t about building a checklist of conditions that look like modern-day Earth. Studying the history of our planet and the evolution of life allows astrobiologists to view periods of time when the Earth was a much different planet than it is today. In this way, we gain a better understanding of the breadth of planets in the universe that could be habitable for life as we know it.

“Our NASA ICAR shows that mapping the evolutionary history of bio-essential elements on Earth can help us predict what life on other worlds might use, and that different abiotic inventories could lead to different biological element choices,” said Kaçar. “Life detection should be metal-aware, redox-aware, and evolution-aware. We should look not just for ‘Earth-like life now,’ but for biochemical strategies that would make sense on a planet with a different history of oxygenation and metal availability.”

For more information on astrobiology at NASA, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/astrobiology

-end-

Karen Fox / Molly Wasser

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery funded by NASA that reveals how early life on Earth utilized the scarce metal molybdenum over 3 billion years ago. This finding advances our understanding of the evolution of life and biochemical processes, with broad implications for astrobiology and the search for life beyond Earth. The study is detailed, focused, and highlights a meaningful breakthrough in knowledge about life's origins and planetary habitability.
articles.personality_title: NASA study reveals early life used rare metal molybdenum over 3 billion years ago
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how life on Earth began and what materials it needed to survive. One mystery was how ancient life sped up important chemical reactions when some key metals, like molybdenum, were very rare in the environment.

**What happened** – A NASA-funded research team led by Betül Kaçar found that over 3 billion years ago, early microbes already used molybdenum to help chemical reactions in their cells. This discovery, published in Nature Communications, shows that life used molybdenum much earlier than previously thought, even when it was scarce in ancient oceans.

**Impact** – This finding changes our understanding of early life’s chemistry. It shows that despite molybdenum’s rarity, ancient life found ways to use it because it helped important processes like the nitrogen cycle. It also suggests early life used both molybdenum and tungsten metals, rather than switching from one to the other. This helps scientists better understand how life evolved and what conditions it can survive.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will use this knowledge to study other planets and moons. Understanding which metals life can use helps in the search for life beyond Earth. Scientists will look for different chemical signs that life might use metals in new ways, depending on a planet’s environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Early life on Earth relied on the rare metal molybdenum more than 3 billion years ago, revealing new clues about how life started and what elements it needs to survive.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude de la NASA révèle que la vie primitive utilisait le métal rare molybdène il y a plus de 3 milliards d'années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment la vie a commencé sur Terre et quels éléments lui étaient nécessaires. Un mystère était de savoir comment la vie ancienne accélérerait des réactions chimiques importantes alors que certains métaux clés, comme le molybdène, étaient très rares.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe financée par la NASA, dirigée par Betül Kaçar, a découvert qu’il y a plus de 3 milliards d’années, les premiers microbes utilisaient déjà le molybdène pour aider les réactions chimiques dans leurs cellules. Cette découverte, publiée dans Nature Communications, montre que la vie a utilisé le molybdène bien plus tôt qu’on ne le pensait, malgré sa rareté dans les océans anciens.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de la chimie de la vie ancienne. Elle montre que, malgré la rareté du molybdène, la vie ancienne savait l’utiliser car il aidait des processus importants comme le cycle de l’azote. Elle suggère aussi que la vie ancienne utilisait à la fois le molybdène et le tungstène, plutôt que de passer de l’un à l’autre. Cela aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre l’évolution de la vie et les conditions où elle peut survivre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront ces connaissances pour étudier d’autres planètes et lunes. Comprendre quels métaux la vie peut utiliser aide à chercher la vie ailleurs. Les scientifiques chercheront des signes chimiques différents, montrant que la vie pourrait utiliser les métaux de nouvelles façons selon l’environnement d’une planète.

**En une phrase** – La vie ancienne sur Terre dépendait du métal rare molybdène il y a plus de 3 milliards d’années, révélant de nouveaux indices sur les débuts de la vie et les éléments nécessaires à sa survie.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio de la NASA revela que la vida temprana usó el raro metal molibdeno hace más de 3 mil millones de años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado cómo comenzó la vida en la Tierra y qué materiales necesitaba para sobrevivir. Un misterio era cómo la vida antigua aceleraba reacciones químicas importantes cuando algunos metales clave, como el molibdeno, eran muy escasos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo financiado por la NASA, liderado por Betül Kaçar, descubrió que hace más de 3 mil millones de años, los primeros microbios ya usaban molibdeno para ayudar en reacciones químicas dentro de sus células. Este hallazgo, publicado en Nature Communications, muestra que la vida usó molibdeno mucho antes de lo que se pensaba, aunque era raro en los océanos antiguos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia nuestra comprensión de la química de la vida antigua. Muestra que, a pesar de la escasez del molibdeno, la vida antigua lo usó porque ayudaba en procesos importantes como el ciclo del nitrógeno. También sugiere que la vida temprana usó tanto molibdeno como tungsteno, en lugar de cambiar de uno a otro. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo evolucionó la vida y en qué condiciones puede sobrevivir.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán esta información para estudiar otros planetas y lunas. Entender qué metales puede usar la vida ayuda a buscarla fuera de la Tierra. Los científicos buscarán diferentes señales químicas que muestren que la vida podría usar metales de formas nuevas, según el ambiente del planeta.

**Resumen en una frase** – La vida temprana en la Tierra dependió del raro metal molibdeno hace más de 3 mil millones de años, revelando nuevas pistas sobre cómo comenzó la vida y qué elementos necesita para sobrevivir.
articles.image_prompt: An ancient underwater scene depicting glowing hydrothermal vents releasing trace amounts of shimmering molybdenum and tungsten particles into the dark primordial ocean, with stylized molecular structures of enzymes subtly integrated into the water currents, all rendered in warm, earthy tones with soft, natural lighting to evoke the dawn of life on early Earth.

articles.title: Webb space telescope reveals a scorching “super-Earth” that looks like Mercury
articles.summary: A scorching, airless world just 48 light-years away is offering scientists a rare glimpse into the geology of distant planets. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, researchers studied LHS 3844 b—a tidally locked “super-Earth” with a permanent dayside hot enough to melt metal—and discovered it’s a dark, barren rock with no atmosphere.
articles.content:
Using MIRI (Mid Infrared Instrument) on board the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), an international team led by former MPIA (Max Planck Institute for Astronomy, Heidelberg, Germany) PhD student Sebastian Zieba (Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian, Cambridge, USA) and Laura Kreidberg, MPIA Director and study PI (principal investigator), investigated the surface composition of the rocky exoplanet LHS 3844 b. Moving beyond studies of atmospheres, this work focuses on the geology of planets orbiting other stars, offering deeper insight into their nature. The findings have been published in the journal Nature Astronomy.

LHS 3844 b is a rocky world about 30% larger than Earth that circles a cool red dwarf star in just under 11 hours. It orbits extremely close to its star, only about three stellar diameters above the surface. The planet is tidally locked, meaning one side permanently faces the star while the other remains in darkness. The dayside reaches an average temperature of about 1000 Kelvin (approximately 725 Degrees Celsius or 1340 Degrees Fahrenheit). The system lies relatively close to Earth at a distance of 48.5 light-years (14.9 parsecs).

"Thanks to the amazing sensitivity of JWST, we can detect light coming directly from the surface of this distant rocky planet. We see a dark, hot, barren rock, devoid of any atmosphere," said Laura Kreidberg, MPIA.

Its dark appearance suggests it may resemble an enlarged version of the Moon or Mercury. This conclusion comes from analyzing infrared radiation emitted by the planet's hot dayside. Scientists cannot directly image the planet. Instead, they measure subtle changes in brightness from the combined light of the star and the orbiting planet as it moves.

MIRI examined infrared emission between 5 and 12 micrometers by splitting the light into smaller wavelength intervals and measuring the intensity in each. This process creates a spectrum, which is similar to a rainbow that reveals how light is distributed across different wavelengths. Earlier observations from the Spitzer Space Telescope provided additional data that strengthened the analysis.

Ruling Out an Earth-Like Crust

Research into exoplanet geology builds on knowledge gained from studying Earth and other rocky bodies in the Solar System. The team compared their observations with computer models and libraries of known rocks and minerals from Earth, the Moon, and Mars. These comparisons showed that LHS 3844 b does not have a crust similar to Earth's, which is typically rich in silicate minerals like granite.

This finding is not unexpected, since Earth is unique in the Solar System for having such a crust. Still, it provides clues about the planet's past. On Earth, silicate-rich crusts form through long-term processes involving tectonic activity and often require water. Rock is repeatedly melted and recycled, allowing lighter materials to rise and form the crust.

"Since LHS 3844 b lacks such a silicate crust, one may conclude that Earth-like plate tectonics does not apply to this planet, or it is ineffective," says Sebastian Zieba. "This planet likely only contains little water."

A Basalt-Rich Surface

Instead of granite-like material, the data point to a surface made of basalt or mantle-like rock, similar to volcanic material found on Earth or the Moon. The researchers carried out a detailed statistical comparison between the observed spectrum and various possible mixtures of minerals.

They found that large areas of solid basalt or magmatic rock best match the data. These rocks are rich in magnesium and iron and may contain minerals such as olivine. Broken rock fragments like gravel also fit reasonably well, while fine powders or dust alone do not match the observations due to their brighter appearance.

Without an atmosphere to shield it, the planet is constantly exposed to intense radiation from its star and impacts from meteorites. These processes gradually break down rock and alter its surface.

"It turns out, these processes not only slowly dissolve hard rocks into regolith, a layer of fine grains or powder as found on the Moon," explains Zieba. "They also darken the layer by adding iron and carbon, making the regolith's properties more consistent with the observations."

Fresh Lava or Ancient Dust

The data support two possible explanations for the planet's surface. One scenario suggests a landscape dominated by solid basaltic rock that is relatively fresh. In this case, recent geological activity such as widespread volcanism would have resurfaced the planet.

The second scenario also involves a dark surface, but one shaped by long-term exposure to space. Over time, weathering would create extensive layers of darkened regolith, similar to the dusty surface seen on the Moon or Mercury. This interpretation implies that the planet has been geologically inactive for a long period.

Searching for Signs of Activity

These two possibilities differ mainly in whether the planet is still geologically active. On Earth and other active worlds, volcanic processes release gases into the surrounding environment. One such gas is sulfur dioxide (SO2), which is commonly associated with volcanism.

If LHS 3844 b were currently active, MIRI would likely have detected this gas. However, no such signal was found. This absence suggests that recent volcanic activity is unlikely, making the weathered, inactive surface scenario more plausible. If correct, the planet may closely resemble Mercury.

To resolve this question, the team is pursuing further observations with JWST. These new measurements aim to detect subtle differences in how solid rock and loose material emit and reflect light. The way light is emitted at different angles depends on surface texture, which can reveal whether the surface is smooth rock or rough, powdery material. This method has already been used successfully to study asteroids in the Solar System.

"We are confident the same technique will allow us to clarify the nature of LHS 3844 b's crust and, in the future, other rocky exoplanets," concludes Kreidberg.

Additional Information

Laura Kreidberg is the only MPIA astronomer involved in this study.

Other researchers were: Sebastian Zieba (Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian, Cambridge, USA), Brandon P. Coy (Department of the Geophysical Sciences, University of Chicago, USA), Aaron Bello-Arufe (Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, USA [JPL]), Kimberly Paragas (Division of Geological and Planetary Sciences, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, USA), Xintong Lyu (Peking University, Beijing, China), Renyu Hu (The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, USA and JPL), Aishwarya Iyer (NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, USA), Kay Wohlfarth (Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany)

The JWST observations used in this study were conducted as part of GO program #1846 (PI: Laura Kreidberg, co-PI: Renyu Hu) titled "A Search for Signatures of Volcanism and Geodynamics on the Hot Rocky Exoplanet LHS 3844 b."

The MIRI consortium comprises the ESA (European Space Agency) member states: Belgium, Denmark, France, Germany, Ireland, the Netherlands, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom. National science organisations fund the consortium's work -- in Germany, the Max Planck Society (MPG) and the German Aerospace Center (DLR). Participating German institutions include the Max Planck Institute for Astronomy in Heidelberg, the University of Cologne, and Hensoldt AG in Oberkochen, formerly Carl Zeiss Optronics.

The James Webb Space Telescope is the world's leading observatory for space research. It is an international programme led by NASA and its partners ESA and CSA (Canadian Space Agency).

The Spitzer Space Telescope was operated by the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, under a contract with NASA.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery using the James Webb Space Telescope, providing new insights into the geology of a rocky exoplanet. This breakthrough advances understanding of planetary formation and characteristics beyond our solar system, with broad implications for astronomy and planetary science. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful real-world impact in space exploration and knowledge.
articles.personality_title: James Webb telescope uncovers rocky, Mercury-like planet nearby
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are studying planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets, to learn what they are made of and how they form. The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), a powerful space observatory launched by NASA and its partners, helps researchers see these distant worlds in new ways.

**What happened** – Using JWST’s special instrument called MIRI, an international team looked at a rocky planet named LHS 3844 b, located about 48 light-years from Earth. This planet is a “super-Earth,” about 30% bigger than Earth, and it orbits very close to its star, making one side extremely hot—hot enough to melt metal. The scientists found that LHS 3844 b has no atmosphere and has a dark, rocky surface similar to Mercury or the Moon.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows the planet’s surface is different from Earth’s. Instead of an Earth-like crust made of light minerals like granite, LHS 3844 b’s surface is made of basalt or volcanic rock. This means it likely doesn’t have plate tectonics or much water. Understanding this helps scientists learn how rocky planets can be very different, even if they are similar in size to Earth.

**What's next step** – The researchers will continue using JWST to study the planet’s surface more closely. They want to find out if the planet is still geologically active with volcanoes or if its surface is old and covered in dust like Mercury. This will help them understand how planets change over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope, scientists found a nearby super-Earth with a hot, dark, airless surface made of volcanic rock, revealing new details about rocky planets beyond our solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb révèle une super-Terre rocheuse proche de Mercure
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des planètes situées en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, pour comprendre leur composition et leur formation. Le télescope spatial James Webb (JWST), un puissant observatoire spatial lancé par la NASA et ses partenaires, permet aux chercheurs d’observer ces mondes lointains d’une nouvelle manière.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à l’instrument spécial MIRI du JWST, une équipe internationale a étudié une planète rocheuse nommée LHS 3844 b, située à environ 48 années-lumière de la Terre. Cette planète est une « super-Terre », environ 30 % plus grande que la Terre, et elle orbite très près de son étoile, ce qui rend une de ses faces extrêmement chaude – assez pour faire fondre du métal. Les scientifiques ont découvert que LHS 3844 b n’a pas d’atmosphère et possède une surface sombre et rocheuse, semblable à Mercure ou à la Lune.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la surface de la planète est différente de celle de la Terre. Au lieu d’une croûte terrestre composée de minéraux légers comme le granite, la surface de LHS 3844 b est faite de basalte ou de roche volcanique. Cela signifie probablement qu’elle n’a pas de tectonique des plaques ni beaucoup d’eau. Ces informations aident les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la diversité des planètes rocheuses, même si elles ont une taille similaire à la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à utiliser le JWST pour étudier plus précisément la surface de la planète. Ils veulent savoir si la planète est encore géologiquement active avec des volcans ou si sa surface est ancienne et recouverte de poussière, comme Mercure. Cela leur permettra de mieux comprendre l’évolution des planètes.

**Phrase-clé** – Grâce au télescope James Webb, les scientifiques ont découvert une super-Terre proche avec une surface chaude, sombre et sans atmosphère faite de roche volcanique, révélant de nouveaux détails sur les planètes rocheuses au-delà de notre système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio James Webb descubre un super-Tierra rocosa similar a Mercurio cerca de nosotros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas, para entender de qué están hechos y cómo se forman. El telescopio espacial James Webb (JWST), un poderoso observatorio lanzado por la NASA y sus socios, ayuda a los investigadores a observar estos mundos lejanos de nuevas formas.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el instrumento especial MIRI del JWST, un equipo internacional observó un planeta rocoso llamado LHS 3844 b, ubicado a unos 48 años luz de la Tierra. Este planeta es una “super-Tierra”, aproximadamente un 30 % más grande que la Tierra, y orbita muy cerca de su estrella, lo que hace que un lado sea extremadamente caliente, lo suficiente para fundir metal. Los científicos descubrieron que LHS 3844 b no tiene atmósfera y tiene una superficie oscura y rocosa, similar a Mercurio o la Luna.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que la superficie del planeta es diferente a la de la Tierra. En lugar de una corteza terrestre hecha de minerales claros como el granito, la superficie de LHS 3844 b está compuesta de basalto o roca volcánica. Esto sugiere que probablemente no tiene tectónica de placas ni mucha agua. Entender esto ayuda a los científicos a conocer mejor cómo pueden ser diferentes los planetas rocosos, aunque tengan un tamaño parecido al de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán usando el JWST para estudiar más de cerca la superficie del planeta. Quieren saber si el planeta sigue activo geológicamente con volcanes o si su superficie es antigua y está cubierta de polvo, como Mercurio. Esto les ayudará a entender cómo cambian los planetas con el tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Usando el telescopio James Webb, los científicos encontraron una super-Tierra cercana con una superficie caliente, oscura y sin atmósfera hecha de roca volcánica, revelando nuevos detalles sobre planetas rocosos fuera de nuestro sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a dark, barren rocky exoplanet with a glowing molten surface on one side facing a nearby red dwarf star, showing stark contrast between the scorching, basaltic dayside and the shadowed, cold nightside, with subtle swirling infrared light waves symbolizing the James Webb Space Telescope’s sensitive detection of the planet’s heat and geology.

articles.title: This tiny outer Solar System world has an atmosphere. It shouldn’t
articles.summary: Astronomers have spotted something surprising in the far outer Solar System—a faint, short-lived atmosphere clinging to a tiny icy world that shouldn’t be able to hold one at all. The object, called 2002 XV93, is far smaller than Pluto, yet observations during a rare stellar alignment revealed its presence through a subtle dimming of starlight. Even more puzzling, calculations suggest this atmosphere should vanish within about 1,000 years unless it’s constantly being replenished.
articles.content:
A group of professional and amateur astronomers in Japan has uncovered evidence that a small, distant object in the outer Solar System is surrounded by a thin atmosphere. The finding is surprising because the object is far too small to hold onto gas for long, raising new questions about how and when this atmosphere formed. Future observations will be needed to better understand its composition and origin.

Far beyond Neptune's orbit, thousands of icy bodies known as trans-Neptunian objects (TNOs) circle the Sun. Pluto is the most well-known example and is one of the few with a confirmed thin atmosphere. For most TNOs, however, the combination of extremely low temperatures and weak gravity makes it unlikely for them to retain any gases. As a result, scientists generally expect these distant objects to be airless.

A Rare Stellar Alignment Reveals Clues

Astronomers took advantage of a rare opportunity to test that assumption using a TNO called (612533) 2002 XV 93 . This object, commonly shortened to 2002 XV 93 , measures about 500 km across, much smaller than Pluto, which spans 2,377 km.

On January 10, 2024, 2002 XV 93 passed directly in front of a background star as viewed from Japan. Events like this, known as stellar occultations, allow scientists to study distant objects in detail. If the object has no atmosphere, the star's light should disappear abruptly as it is blocked. If an atmosphere is present, the starlight fades more gradually as it passes through surrounding gas.

Observations Suggest a Thin Atmosphere

A research team led by Ko Arimatsu at NAOJ Ishigakijima Astronomical Observatory observed this event from multiple locations across Japan. Their measurements showed a pattern consistent with the gradual dimming expected when light passes through a thin atmosphere. These results strongly suggest that 2002 XV 93 is surrounded by a layer of gas.

A Short-Lived and Mysterious Origin

Further analysis indicates that this atmosphere should not last long. Calculations suggest it would dissipate in less than 1000 years unless it is continuously replenished. This means the atmosphere must have formed or been renewed relatively recently.

Data from the James Webb Space Telescope provide another piece of the puzzle. Observations show no evidence of frozen gases on the surface that could slowly turn into vapor and sustain an atmosphere. This leaves scientists considering other explanations. One possibility is that material from inside the object was brought to the surface, releasing gas. Another is that a comet impact delivered or released enough material to create a temporary atmosphere.

More observations will be needed to determine which scenario is correct and to better understand how such a small, distant object can briefly hold onto an atmosphere at all.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a thin atmosphere around a small outer Solar System object, challenging existing assumptions about such bodies. This finding has broad implications for planetary science and our understanding of atmospheric formation and retention in the outer Solar System. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful breakthrough in space exploration and astronomy.
articles.personality_title: Tiny distant world 2002 XV93 found to have a surprising thin atmosphere
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Far beyond Neptune, many small icy objects called trans-Neptunian objects (TNOs) orbit the Sun. Most are too small and cold to hold an atmosphere, unlike Pluto, which has a thin one.

**What happened** – On January 10, 2024, astronomers in Japan observed 2002 XV93, a 500 km-wide TNO, pass in front of a star. This rare event let them see the star’s light slowly dim, showing the presence of a thin atmosphere around the object.

**Impact** – This discovery is surprising because 2002 XV93 is much smaller than Pluto and should not be able to hold an atmosphere for long. Scientists estimate the atmosphere would disappear within about 1,000 years unless it is being renewed. This challenges ideas about how small, cold objects keep gases around them.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue watching 2002 XV93 and use tools like the James Webb Space Telescope to learn more about what the atmosphere is made of and how it forms. They want to find out if the atmosphere comes from inside the object or from events like comet impacts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny icy world far from the Sun has a thin atmosphere, revealing new mysteries about how small outer Solar System objects can hold onto gas.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un petit monde lointain, 2002 XV93, possède une atmosphère surprenante
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Bien au-delà de Neptune, de nombreux petits objets glacés appelés objets transneptuniens (TNO) tournent autour du Soleil. La plupart sont trop petits et froids pour retenir une atmosphère, contrairement à Pluton, qui en possède une fine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 janvier 2024, des astronomes au Japon ont observé 2002 XV93, un TNO de 500 km de large, passer devant une étoile. Cet événement rare leur a permis de voir la lumière de l’étoile s’estomper lentement, montrant la présence d’une atmosphère fine autour de l’objet.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est surprenante car 2002 XV93 est beaucoup plus petit que Pluton et ne devrait pas pouvoir garder une atmosphère longtemps. Les scientifiques estiment que l’atmosphère disparaîtrait en moins de 1 000 ans à moins d’être renouvelée. Cela remet en question les idées sur la façon dont les petits objets froids retiennent les gaz.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’observer 2002 XV93 et utiliseront des instruments comme le télescope spatial James Webb pour en savoir plus sur la composition et la formation de cette atmosphère. Ils veulent découvrir si l’atmosphère vient de l’intérieur de l’objet ou d’événements comme des impacts de comètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un petit monde glacé loin du Soleil possède une atmosphère fine, révélant de nouveaux mystères sur la façon dont les petits objets lointains retiennent les gaz.
articles.personality_title_es: Pequeño mundo lejano 2002 XV93 tiene una atmósfera sorprendente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muy lejos de Neptuno, muchos pequeños objetos helados llamados objetos transneptunianos (TNO) orbitan el Sol. La mayoría son demasiado pequeños y fríos para mantener una atmósfera, a diferencia de Plutón, que tiene una fina.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de enero de 2024, astrónomos en Japón observaron a 2002 XV93, un TNO de 500 km de ancho, pasar frente a una estrella. Este evento raro les permitió ver cómo la luz de la estrella se atenuaba lentamente, mostrando la presencia de una atmósfera delgada alrededor del objeto.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es sorprendente porque 2002 XV93 es mucho más pequeño que Plutón y no debería poder mantener una atmósfera por mucho tiempo. Los científicos calculan que la atmósfera desaparecería en menos de 1,000 años a menos que se renueve constantemente. Esto desafía las ideas sobre cómo los objetos pequeños y fríos retienen gases.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán observando 2002 XV93 y usarán herramientas como el telescopio espacial James Webb para aprender más sobre la composición y origen de la atmósfera. Quieren saber si la atmósfera viene del interior del objeto o de eventos como impactos de cometas.

**Frase clave** – Un pequeño mundo helado lejos del Sol tiene una atmósfera delgada, revelando nuevos misterios sobre cómo los objetos pequeños en el sistema solar exterior pueden retener gases.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small icy celestial body in deep space, softly glowing with a delicate, translucent halo representing its thin atmosphere, positioned in front of a distant star whose light gently fades through the gas; in the background, subtle silhouettes of telescopes and starry night skies over Japan symbolize the astronomers’ observations.
